saida_bruta:
Nothing about the appearance of the man who was slowly waking on the sleeping porch of a Dutch Colonial home in the Zenith neighborhood called Floral Heights suggested anything giant-like.

He was George F. Babbitt. In April of 1920 he was forty-six, and he produced nothing especially useful, neither cheese nor boots nor verse, but he was skillful in the profession of selling homes at prices beyond what buyers could comfortably pay.

His broad head was pink, and his brown hair was sparse and brittle. In sleep his face looked childish, in spite of its wrinkles and the red impressions left by spectacles along his nose. He was not stout, yet he was remarkably well nourished; his cheeks were cushioned, and the smooth hand resting uselessly on the khaki blanket was a little swollen. He looked successful, thoroughly married, and without romance; nor was there romance in the porch, overlooking a large elm, two proper lawns, a concrete drive, and a corrugated-metal garage. Still, Babbitt was dreaming again of the fairy child, in a dream more romantic than crimson pagodas beside a silver sea.

Nothing about the appearance of the man who was slowly waking on the sleeping porch of a Dutch Colonial home in the Zenith neighborhood called Floral Heights suggested anything giant-like.

He was George F. Babbitt. In April of 1920 he was forty-six, and he produced nothing especially useful, neither cheese nor boots nor verse, but he was skillful in the profession of selling homes at prices beyond what buyers could comfortably pay.

His broad head was pink, and his brown hair was sparse and brittle. In sleep his face looked childish, in spite of its wrinkles and the red impressions left by spectacles along his nose. He was not stout, yet he was remarkably well nourished; his cheeks were cushioned, and the smooth hand resting uselessly on the khaki blanket was a little swollen. He looked successful, thoroughly married, and without romance; nor was there romance in the porch, overlooking a large elm, two proper lawns, a concrete drive, and a corrugated-metal garage. Still, Babbitt was dreaming again of the fairy child, in a dream more romantic than crimson pagodas beside a silver sea.

saida_bruta:
Nothing in the look of the man gradually stirring on the screened sleeping porch of a Dutch Colonial house in Zenith’s Floral Heights district hinted at anything remotely heroic or immense.

He was George F. Babbitt. In April 1920 he was forty-six years old, and he made nothing of particular value—no cheese, boots, or poetry—yet he was adept at the business of selling houses for sums greater than purchasers could easily afford.

His large head was rosy, his brown hair thin and dry. While asleep, his face seemed boyish despite its creases and the reddish marks made by eyeglasses across his nose. He was not fat, but he was conspicuously well fed; his cheeks were padded, and the smooth hand lying idly on the khaki coverlet was slightly puffy. He appeared prosperous, entirely married, and untouched by romance; nor did romance inhabit the porch, with its view of a great elm, two respectable lawns, a cement driveway, and a sheet-metal garage. Yet Babbitt was once more dreaming of the fairy child, in a vision lovelier than scarlet pagodas beside a shining sea.

saida_bruta:
Nothing in the appearance of the man slowly waking on the screened porch of a Dutch Colonial home in Zenith’s Floral Heights neighborhood suggested anything even faintly heroic or grand.

He was George F. Babbitt. In April 1920, he was forty-six, and he produced nothing of special worth—no cheese, shoes, or verse—though he was skilled in selling homes for prices buyers could scarcely manage.

His broad head was pink, his brown hair sparse and brittle. In sleep, his face looked youthful despite its wrinkles and the red impressions left by spectacles on his nose. He was not overweight, yet plainly well nourished; his cheeks were full, and the smooth hand resting carelessly on the khaki blanket was a little swollen. He looked successful, thoroughly married, and free of romance; nor was there romance in the porch, overlooking a huge elm, two proper lawns, a concrete drive, and a metal garage. Still, Babbitt was again dreaming of the fairy child, in a scene more beautiful than crimson pagodas beside a gleaming sea.

saida_bruta:
Nothing about the man gradually stirring on the screened veranda of a Dutch Colonial house in Zenith’s Floral Heights district hinted at anything remotely noble or magnificent.

He was George F. Babbitt. In April of 1920, he was forty-six years old, and he created nothing especially valuable—no cheese, boots, or poetry—although he was adept at selling houses for sums purchasers could barely afford.

His large head was rosy, his brown hair thin and wiry. As he slept, his face seemed almost boyish despite the creases and the red marks his eyeglasses had pressed into his nose. He was not fat, but obviously well fed; his cheeks were rounded, and the soft hand lying idly on the khaki coverlet was slightly puffed. He appeared prosperous, completely married, and untouched by romance; neither did the porch possess romance, with its view of a great elm, two respectable lawns, a cement driveway, and a tin garage. Yet Babbitt was once more dreaming of the fairy girl, amid a landscape lovelier than scarlet pagodas beside a shining sea.

saida_bruta:
There was nothing in the figure of the man slowly waking on the enclosed porch of a Dutch Colonial home in Zenith’s Floral Heights neighborhood that suggested nobility, grandeur, or any kind of distinction.

He was George F. Babbitt. In April 1920, he was forty-six, and he produced nothing particularly useful—neither cheese nor boots nor poems—though he was skilled at selling homes for prices buyers could scarcely manage.

His broad head was pinkish, and his brown hair was sparse and stiff. In sleep, his face looked nearly youthful, notwithstanding its wrinkles and the red dents where his spectacles had rested on his nose. He was not obese, yet he was plainly well nourished; his cheeks were full, and the relaxed hand on the khaki bedspread was somewhat swollen. He looked successful, thoroughly domesticated, and wholly free of romance; nor was there romance in the porch, overlooking a huge elm, two proper lawns, a concrete drive, and a metal garage. Still, Babbitt was again dreaming of the fairy girl, in scenery more beautiful than crimson pagodas beside a glittering sea.
